Project 1 - World Happiness Report

Ashmita Neupane

2026-05-26

## Introduction
# The data used in this analysis was sourced from Kaggle, originally publishes by the Sustainable Development Solutions Network (SDSN). It covers approximately 150 countries per year from 2017 to 2020, with key variables including happiness score, GDP per capita, social support, and healthy life expectancy. The four yearly datasets were merged and cleaned in R prior to analysis. The goal of this project is to uncover patterns and relationships in global happiness through data visualization.
# 2017
df2017 <- read_csv("2017.csv") %>%
  select(Country, Score = Happiness.Score, Rank = Happiness.Rank,
         GDP = Economy..GDP.per.Capita., Social_Support = Family,
         Life_Exp = Health..Life.Expectancy.) %>%
  mutate(Year = 2017, Region = NA)
## Rows: 155 Columns: 12
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr  (1): Country
## dbl (11): Happiness.Rank, Happiness.Score, Whisker.high, Whisker.low, Econom...
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
# 2018
df2018 <- read_csv("2018.csv") %>%
  select(Country = `Country or region`, Score, Rank = `Overall rank`,
         GDP = `GDP per capita`, Social_Support = `Social support`,
         Life_Exp = `Healthy life expectancy`) %>%
  mutate(Year = 2018, Region = NA)
## Rows: 156 Columns: 9
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (2): Country or region, Perceptions of corruption
## dbl (7): Overall rank, Score, GDP per capita, Social support, Healthy life e...
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
# 2019
df2019 <- read_csv("2019.csv") %>%
  select(Country = `Country or region`, Score, Rank = `Overall rank`,
         GDP = `GDP per capita`, Social_Support = `Social support`,
         Life_Exp = `Healthy life expectancy`) %>%
  mutate(Year = 2019, Region = NA)
## Rows: 156 Columns: 9
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (1): Country or region
## dbl (8): Overall rank, Score, GDP per capita, Social support, Healthy life e...
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
# 2020
df2020 <- read_csv("2020.csv") %>%
  select(Country = `Country name`, Region = `Regional indicator`,
         Score = `Ladder score`, GDP = `Logged GDP per capita`,
         Social_Support = `Social support`,
         Life_Exp = `Healthy life expectancy`) %>%
  mutate(Year = 2020, Rank = rank(-Score))
## Rows: 153 Columns: 20
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr  (2): Country name, Regional indicator
## dbl (18): Ladder score, Standard error of ladder score, upperwhisker, lowerw...
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
# Stack all years
df_all <- sqldf("
  SELECT Country, Score, Rank, GDP, Social_Support, Life_Exp, Year, Region
  FROM df2017
  UNION ALL
  SELECT Country, Score, Rank, GDP, Social_Support, Life_Exp, Year, Region
  FROM df2018
  UNION ALL
  SELECT Country, Score, Rank, GDP, Social_Support, Life_Exp, Year, Region
  FROM df2019
  UNION ALL
  SELECT Country, Score, Rank, GDP, Social_Support, Life_Exp, Year, Region
  FROM df2020
")

df_all <- df_all %>%
  group_by(Year) %>%
  mutate(GDP_scaled = (GDP - min(GDP, na.rm = TRUE)) /
           (max(GDP, na.rm = TRUE) - min(GDP, na.rm = TRUE))) %>%
  ungroup()
# Get min and max GDP
df_all <- df_all %>%
  group_by(Year) %>%
  mutate(GDP_scaled = (GDP - min(GDP)) / (max(GDP) - min(GDP))) %>%
  ungroup()
# Q1 - Top 10 Happiest Countries in 2020

# Filter 2020 data and get top 10
top10_2020 <- df_all %>%
  filter(Year == 2020) %>%
  arrange(desc(Score)) %>%
  slice(1:10)

print(top10_2020)
## # A tibble: 10 × 9
##    Country     Score  Rank   GDP Social_Support Life_Exp  Year Region GDP_scaled
##    <chr>       <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>          <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>
##  1 Finland      7.81     1  10.6          0.954     71.9  2020 Weste…      0.836
##  2 Denmark      7.65     2  10.8          0.956     72.4  2020 Weste…      0.864
##  3 Switzerland  7.56     3  11.0          0.943     74.1  2020 Weste…      0.905
##  4 Iceland      7.50     4  10.8          0.975     73    2020 Weste…      0.863
##  5 Norway       7.49     5  11.1          0.952     73.2  2020 Weste…      0.927
##  6 Netherlands  7.45     6  10.8          0.939     72.3  2020 Weste…      0.871
##  7 Sweden       7.35     7  10.8          0.926     72.6  2020 Weste…      0.860
##  8 New Zealand  7.30     8  10.5          0.949     73.2  2020 North…      0.808
##  9 Austria      7.29     9  10.7          0.928     73.0  2020 Weste…      0.857
## 10 Luxembourg   7.24    10  11.5          0.907     72.6  2020 Weste…      1
# Create barplot
ggplot(top10_2020, aes(x = reorder(Country, -Score), y = Score)) + 
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue", width = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Happiest Countries in 2020",
       x = "Country",
       y = "Happiness Score") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

# Q1 Analysis
# The 2020 data reveals that Finland ranks as the happiest country in the world, followed closely by Denmark, Switzerland, and Iceland. All top 10 countries are from Western Europe or the broader Western world, with New Zealand being the only non-European country. Finland’s top ranking is often attributed to its strong social safety net, high quality public education, low corruption, and high levels of trust in government and institutions. The scores are tightly clustered between 7.2 and 7.8, suggesting that the happiest nations share similarly strong social, economic, and institutional foundations. The relatively small differences in scores among the top 10 highlight that happiness at the highest levels is driven by a combination of mutually reinforcing factors rather than any single standout characteristic. 
# Q2 - Happiness score over the 4 years for six Countries

# Filter the 6 countries
six_countries <- df_all %>%
  filter(Country %in% 
           c("China", "India", "United States", "Indonesia", "Japan", "Russia"))

head(six_countries)
## # A tibble: 6 × 9
##   Country      Score  Rank   GDP Social_Support Life_Exp  Year Region GDP_scaled
##   <chr>        <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>          <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>
## 1 United Stat…  6.99    14 1.55           1.42     0.774  2017 <NA>        0.827
## 2 Russia        5.96    49 1.28           1.47     0.547  2017 <NA>        0.685
## 3 Japan         5.92    51 1.42           1.44     0.913  2017 <NA>        0.757
## 4 China         5.27    79 1.08           1.16     0.741  2017 <NA>        0.578
## 5 Indonesia     5.26    81 0.996          1.27     0.492  2017 <NA>        0.532
## 6 India         4.32   122 0.792          0.754    0.455  2017 <NA>        0.423
# Create line plot
ggplot(six_countries, aes(x = Year, y = Score, color = Country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Happiness Score Over Time (2017-2020)",
       x = "Year",
       y = "Happiness Score",
       color = "Country") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2017, 2018, 2019, 2020))

# Q2 Analysis
# Among the world’s six most populous nations, the United States consistently records the highest happiness score, remaining stable around 6.9-7.0 throughout the four-year period. Japan and Russia show a gradual decline, while Indonesia remains relatively stable with minor fluctuations. China shows a slight downward trend over the period. Most strikingly, India experiences a sharp and consistent decline from approximately 4.3 in 2017 to 3.5 in 2020, the steepest drop among all six countries. This decline may reflect the economic slowdown and rising inequality reported during this period, as well as disruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020. Overall, none of the six most populous nations rank among the top 10 happiest countries, suggesting that population size and happiness do not go hand in hand. 
# Q3 - Countries consistently in top 10 across all 4 years

# Find top 10 for each year
top10_each_year <- df_all %>%
  group_by(Year) %>%
  arrange(desc(Score)) %>%
  slice(1:10) %>%
  ungroup()

# Find countries that appear in top 10 all 4 years
consistent_top10 <- top10_each_year %>%
  group_by(Country) %>%
  summarise(years_in_top10 = n()) %>% #count how many years each country appears
  filter(years_in_top10 == 4) %>% #keep only those in top 10 all 4 years
  arrange(desc(years_in_top10))

# View the result
print(consistent_top10)
## # A tibble: 8 × 2
##   Country     years_in_top10
##   <chr>                <int>
## 1 Denmark                  4
## 2 Finland                  4
## 3 Iceland                  4
## 4 Netherlands              4
## 5 New Zealand              4
## 6 Norway                   4
## 7 Sweden                   4
## 8 Switzerland              4
# Visualize the result
ggplot(consistent_top10, aes(x = reorder(Country, years_in_top10),
                             y = years_in_top10)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue", width = 0.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Countries Consistently in Top 10 (2017-2020)",
       x = "Country",
       y = "Number of Years in Top 10")

# Q3 Analysis
# Only 8 countries maintained a top 10 ranking across all four years: Finland, Denmark, Iceland, Norway, Netherlands, Switzerland, Sweden, and New Zealand. Notably, all 8 are either Nordic or Western European nations, with New Zealand being the only exception. This remarkable consistency suggests that happiness at the top is not a product of short-term circumstances but rather reflects deeply embedded structural advantages – strong social safety nets, high GDP per capita, trusted institutions, and high levels of personal freedom. The fact that only 8 countries achieved this distinction also indicated that the remaining top 10 spots rotated among several other countries across the years. 
# Q4 - GDP vs Happiness Score Scatter Plot
ggplot(df_all, aes(x = GDP_scaled, y = Score, color = factor(Year))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 2) +
  labs(title = "GDP per Capita vs Happiness Score (2017-2020)",
       x = "GDP per Capita (Normalized 0-1)",
       y = "Happiness Score",
       color = "Year")

# Q4 Analysis
# The scatter plot reveals a strong positive correlation between GDP per capita and happiness score across all four years. Countries with higher normalized GDP consistently cluster in the upper right of the plot with higher happiness scores, while lower GDP countries cluster in the lower left. This relationship is consistent across all four years, the colored points for 2017, 2018, 2019, and 2020 follow the same upward trend without significant divergence. This suggests that GDP-happiness relationship is stable over time and not driven by any single year’s conditions. However, notable spread exits at higher GDP levels, indicating that while wealth is a strong predictor of happiness, it does not guarantee it, other factors like social support and freedom also play important roles. GDP values were normalized using min-max scaling within each year to ensure comparability across the four datasets. 
# Q5 - Happiness score distribution by region

# Load GNI2014 from treemap package
data("GNI2014", package = "treemap")

head(GNI2014)
##   iso3          country     continent population    GNI
## 3  BMU          Bermuda North America      67837 106140
## 4  NOR           Norway        Europe    4676305 103630
## 5  QAT            Qatar          Asia     833285  92200
## 6  CHE      Switzerland        Europe    7604467  88120
## 7  MAC Macao SAR, China          Asia     559846  76270
## 8  LUX       Luxembourg        Europe     491775  75990
# Get 2020 data with regions
df2020_regions <- df_all %>%
  filter(Year == 2020) %>%
  select(Country, Score, Region)

# Check how many regions we have
table(df2020_regions$Region)
## 
##         Central and Eastern Europe Commonwealth of Independent States 
##                                 17                                 12 
##                          East Asia        Latin America and Caribbean 
##                                  6                                 21 
##       Middle East and North Africa              North America and ANZ 
##                                 17                                  4 
##                         South Asia                     Southeast Asia 
##                                  7                                  9 
##                 Sub-Saharan Africa                     Western Europe 
##                                 39                                 21
# Create the Violin Plot
ggplot(df2020_regions, aes(x = Region, y = Score, fill = Region)) +
  geom_violin(trim = FALSE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.1, fill = "white", alpha = 0.7,
               outlier.shape = 20, outlier.fill = "white") +
  geom_point(stat = "summary", fun = "median",
             color = "white", size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Happiness Scores by Region (2020)",
       x = "Region",
       y = "Happiness Score") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 7),
        legend.position = "none")

# Q5 Analysis
# The violin plot reveals significant variation in happiness both between and within world regions. North America and ANZ (Australia and New Zealand) show the highest median happiness score with an extremely narrow distribution, indicating consistently high happiness across its few countries. Western Europe also scores highly with a wider spread, reflecting some variation among its many member nations. In contrast, Sub-Saharan Africa shows the lowest median score with a wide distribution, suggesting substantial inequality in happiness across the region. The Middle East and North Africa region displays the widest overall spread, with scores ranging from under 3 to nearly 8, reflecting the diverse political and economic circumstances of countries withing this region. South Asia and Southeast Asia cluster in the middle range with moderate variation. 
# Q6 - Relation between Social Support and Life Expectancy 

# Filter 2020 data
df2020_bubble <- df_all %>%
  filter(Year == 2020)

# Create bubble plot
ggplot(df2020_bubble, aes(x = Life_Exp, y = Social_Support, 
                           size = Score, color = Region)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = "Social Support vs Life Expectancy (2020)",
       x = "Life Expectancy",
       y = "Social Support",
       size = "Happiness Score",
       color = "Region") +
  theme(legend.position = "right")

# Q6 Analysis
# The bubble plot demonstrates that social support and life expectancy jointly contribute to national happiness in a clear and meaningful way. Countries in the top right of the plot, those with both high life expectancy (above 70) and strong social support (above 0.85) consistently display the largest bubbles, indicating the highest happiness scores. These countries are predominantly from Western Europe and North America and ANZ. Sub-Saharan African nations cluster in the lower left with lower life expectancy, lower social support, and smaller bubbles reflecting lower happiness scores. This visualization strongly suggests that happiness is not driven by a single factor but rather emerges from the combination of physical wellbeing and social connection working together. Countries that score well on both dimensions are almost universally among the happiest in the world. 
# Q7 - Treemap for 2020

# Filter 2020 data
df2020_tree <- df_all %>%
  filter(Year == 2020) %>%
  arrange(Rank)

head(df2020_tree)
## # A tibble: 6 × 9
##   Country     Score  Rank   GDP Social_Support Life_Exp  Year Region  GDP_scaled
##   <chr>       <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>          <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <chr>        <dbl>
## 1 Finland      7.81     1  10.6          0.954     71.9  2020 Wester…      0.836
## 2 Denmark      7.65     2  10.8          0.956     72.4  2020 Wester…      0.864
## 3 Switzerland  7.56     3  11.0          0.943     74.1  2020 Wester…      0.905
## 4 Iceland      7.50     4  10.8          0.975     73    2020 Wester…      0.863
## 5 Norway       7.49     5  11.1          0.952     73.2  2020 Wester…      0.927
## 6 Netherlands  7.45     6  10.8          0.939     72.3  2020 Wester…      0.871
# Create treemap
treemap(df2020_tree,
        index = "Country",          
        vSize = "Score",      
        vColor = "Rank",            
        type = "value", 
        palette = "-RdYlGn",       
        title = "World Happiness Treemap 2020",
        title.legend = "Happiness Rank",
        fontsize.labels = 8,      
        fontsize.title = 14)

# Q7 Analysis
# The treemap provides an intuitive visual summary of global happiness in 2020, where each rectangle represents a country, its size reflects the happiness score, and its color indicated the numerical rank – lighter yellow for the happiest and darker red for least happy. Finland, Iceland, and other Nordic countries occupy the largest, lightest boxes in the upper left, confirming their dominant position in global happiness rankings. Moving toward the right, boxes become smaller and progressively darker, transitioning through orange toward deep red for the least happy nations such as Chad, Haiti, and India. The treemap shows both the magnitude of happiness scores and the relative ranking of countries in a single unified visualization, making it easy to compare countries across the full spectrum of happiness immediately. 
# Q8 - Choropleth map colored by the happiness score

# Load world map and country reference data
world_map <- map_data("world")
data("countryref", package = "CoordinateCleaner")

head(countryref)
##   iso3 iso2 adm1_code          name    type centroid.lon centroid.lat capital
## 1  AFG   AF       AFG   Afghanistan country       65.000       33.000   Kabul
## 2  AFG   AF       AFG   Afghanistan country       66.000       33.000   Kabul
## 3  AFG   AF       AFG   Afghanistan country       65.216       33.677   Kabul
## 4  ALA   AX       ALA Aland Islands country       19.952       60.198    <NA>
## 5  ALA   AX       ALA Aland Islands country       20.000       60.250    <NA>
## 6  ALB   AL       ALB       Albania country       20.000       41.000  Tirana
##   capital.lon capital.lat area_sqkm uncertaintyRadiusMeters    source
## 1       69.18       34.52 642181.62                     301 geolocate
## 2       69.18       34.52 642181.62                     301 geolocate
## 3       69.18       34.52 642181.62                    <NA>      <NA>
## 4          NA          NA        NA                    <NA>      <NA>
## 5          NA          NA        NA                    <NA>      <NA>
## 6       19.82       41.32  28335.85                     301 geolocate
# Get 2020 happiness scores
df2020_map <- df_all %>%
  filter(Year == 2020) %>%
  select(Country, Score)

# Join happiness data with world map
map_data_joined <- world_map %>%
  left_join(df2020_map, by = c("region" = "Country"))

# Create Choropleth Map
ggplot(map_data_joined, aes(x = long, y = lat, 
                             group = group, fill = Score)) +
  geom_polygon(color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 1.25) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "red", high = "green", 
                      name = "Happiness Score",
                      na.value = "grey80") +
  labs(title = "Choropleth Map of Happiness Scores by Region") +
  theme_void()

# Q8 Analysis
# The choropleth map provides a geographical perspective on global happiness in 2020, with green indicating higher happiness scores and red indicating lower scores. A clear geographic pattern emerges, happiness is highest in North America, Western Europe, and Australia, which appear in bright green. South America and Eastern Europe show moderate happiness levels in yellow-green tones. The Middle East, Central Asia, and much of Africa appear in orange and red, reflecting lower happiness scores. Afghanistan stands out as one of the darkest red countries, consistent with its position near the bottom of global happiness rankings. Grey countries indicate missing data. This geographic clustering strongly suggests that regional factors including political stability, economic development, cultural norms, and access to social services play a significant role in shaping national happiness beyond individual country characteristics. 
## Appendix

# Data Source
# Mathurinache. (n.d.). World Happiness Report 2017–2020. Kaggle. Retrieved from https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/mathurinache/world-happiness-report

# Original data: Helliwell, J., Layard, R., & Sachs, J. (2020). World Happiness Report 2020. Sustainable Development Solutions Network. https://worldhappiness.report/

# Data Description
#The dataset covers approximately 150 countries per year across 2017-2020. Key variables used in this analysis include happiness score (Cantril ladder), GDP per capita, social support, healthy life expectancy, and regional classification. GDP values were normalized using min-max scaling within each year to ensure comparability. The four yearly datasets were merged using the sqldf package in R using SQL UNION ALL syntax.
